questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: If molality of the dilute solution is doubled, the value of molal depression constant (K) will be
questions.solution: Molal depression constant \(K_f\) is a property of the solvent only, defined as \(K_f = \frac{RT_f^2 M}{1000 \Delta H_f}\). It does not depend on molality of the solution. Common mistake: confusing \(\Delta T_f = K_f \times m\) (where \(\Delta T_f\) changes with molality) with \(K_f\) itself.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is dependent on temperature?
questions.solution: Molarity \(= \frac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{volume of solution (L)}}\) depends on volume, which changes with temperature. Mole fraction, weight percentage, and molality are mass-based and temperature-independent. Common mistake: thinking all concentration units are temperature-independent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Of the following 0.10 \(m\) aqueous solutions, which one will exhibit the largest freezing point depression?
questions.solution: \(\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m\). The van't Hoff factor \(i\) for \(Al_2(SO_4)_3\) is 5 (2 Al\(^{3+}\) + 3 SO\(_4^{2-}\)), giving the largest \(\Delta T_f\). Common mistake: choosing KCl (\(i=2\)) or \(K_2SO_4\) (\(i=3\)) without considering \(Al_2(SO_4)_3\) (\(i=5\)).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the molality of a solution containing 200 mg of urea (molar mass = 60 g mol\(^{-1}\)) dissolved in 40 g of water?
questions.solution: Molality \(m = \frac{w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1}\). Here \(w_2 = 0.2\) g, \(M_2 = 60\), \(w_1 = 40\) g: \(m = \frac{0.2 \times 1000}{60 \times 40} = \frac{200}{2400} = 0.0833 \approx 0.0825\). Common mistake: not converting mg to g before calculation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: If \(M\), \(W\) and \(V\) represent molar mass of solute, mass of solute and volume of solution in litres respectively, which among the following equations is true?
questions.solution: Osmotic pressure \(\pi V = nRT\), where \(n = \frac{W}{M}\). Substituting: \(\pi = \frac{WRT}{VM}\), i.e., \(\pi = \frac{TWR}{VM}\). Common mistake: inverting \(M\) and \(W\) in the formula.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The relation between solubility of a gas in liquid at constant temperature and external pressure is stated by which law?
questions.solution: Henry's law states that at constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the solution: \(p = K_H \cdot x\). Common mistake: confusing Henry's law with Raoult's law (which applies to vapour pressure of volatile components).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Molar Mass from Freezing Point Depression
questions.question: What is the molar mass of solute when 5 gram solute dissolved in 70 gram solvent lowers its freezing point by 2.5 K? (Cryoscopic constant for solvent is 4.9 K kg mol\(^{-1}\))
questions.solution: Using \(M_2 = \frac{K_f \times w_2 \times 1000}{\Delta T_f \times w_1} = \frac{4.9 \times 5 \times 1000}{2.5 \times 70} = \frac{24500}{175} = 140\) g mol\(^{-1}\). Common mistake: using volume instead of mass of solvent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Calculate the relative lowering of vapour pressure if the vapour pressure of benzene and vapour pressure of solution of non volatile solute in benzene are 640 mmHg and 590 mmHg respectively at same temperature.
questions.solution: Relative lowering of VP \(= \frac{P^0 - P_s}{P^0} = \frac{640 - 590}{640} = \frac{50}{640} \approx 0.078\). Common mistake: using \(P_s\) in the denominator instead of \(P^0\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Calculate the depression in freezing point of solution when 4 g non-volatile solute of molar mass 126 g mol\(^{-1}\) dissolved in 80 mL water. [Cryoscopic constant of water = 1.86 K kg mol\(^{-1}\)]
questions.solution: \(\Delta T_f = K_f \times \frac{w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1} = 1.86 \times \frac{4 \times 1000}{126 \times 80} = \frac{7440}{10080} \approx 0.74\) K. Common mistake: using volume in litres instead of mass in grams for solvent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Molar Mass from Boiling Point Elevation
questions.question: A solution of non-volatile solute is obtained by dissolving 3.5 g in 100 g solvent has boiling point elevation 0.35 K. Calculate the molar mass of solute. (Molal elevation constant = 2.5 K kg mol\(^{-1}\))
questions.solution: \(M_2 = \frac{K_b \times w_2 \times 1000}{\Delta T_b \times w_1} = \frac{2.5 \times 3.5 \times 1000}{0.35 \times 100} = \frac{8750}{35} = 250\) g mol\(^{-1}\). Common mistake: not multiplying by 1000 when \(w_1\) is in grams.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The mass of glucose that should be dissolved in 50 g of water in order to produce the same lowering of vapour pressure as is produced by dissolving 1 g of urea in the same quantity of water is
questions.solution: Equal lowering of VP means equal mole fractions, hence equal moles. Moles of urea \(= \frac{1}{60}\). Moles of glucose needed \(= \frac{1}{60}\). Mass \(= \frac{1}{60} \times 180 = 3\) g. Common mistake: comparing masses directly instead of moles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Raoult's Law - Vapour Pressure of Pure Component
questions.question: Vapour pressure of pure 'A' is 70 mm of Hg at 25°C. It forms an ideal solution with 'B' in which mole fraction of A is 0.8. If the vapour pressure of the solution is 84 mm of Hg at 25°C, the vapour pressure of pure 'B' at 25°C is
questions.solution: By Raoult's law: \(P_{total} = P_A^0 x_A + P_B^0 x_B\). \(84 = 70 \times 0.8 + P_B^0 \times 0.2\). \(84 - 56 = 0.2 P_B^0\), so \(P_B^0 = 140\) mm. Common mistake: forgetting \(x_B = 1 - x_A = 0.2\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: 3 g of urea is dissolved in 45 g of H\(_2\)O. The relative lowering in vapour pressure is
questions.solution: Moles of urea \(= \frac{3}{60} = 0.05\); moles of water \(= \frac{45}{18} = 2.5\). \(\frac{\Delta P}{P^0} = \frac{n_2}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{0.05}{2.5 + 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{2.55} \approx 0.02\). Common mistake: not adding \(n_2\) to denominator.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Relation Between Elevation and Depression Constants
questions.question: After adding non-volatile solute freezing point of water decreases to \(-0.186°C\). Calculate \(\Delta T_b\) if \(K_f = 1.86\) K kg mol\(^{-1}\) and \(K_b = 0.521\) K kg mol\(^{-1}\)
questions.solution: Molality \(m = \frac{\Delta T_f}{K_f} = \frac{0.186}{1.86} = 0.1\) mol kg\(^{-1}\). Then \(\Delta T_b = K_b \times m = 0.521 \times 0.1 = 0.0521\) K. Common mistake: using 0.186 directly without dividing by \(K_f\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following aqueous solutions has the highest freezing point?
questions.solution: Higher freezing point depression \(\Rightarrow\) lower freezing point. Effective concentration: 0.01M NaCl: \(0.02i\); 0.1M Sucrose: 0.1; 0.01M Na\(_2\)SO\(_4\): 0.03; 0.1M NaCl: 0.2. Lowest effective concentration (0.02) has highest freezing point. Common mistake: ignoring \(i\) for electrolytes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When an electrolyte is dissociated in solution, the van't Hoff factor (i) is
questions.solution: When an electrolyte dissociates, it produces more particles than originally present, so effective number of particles increases: \(i = \frac{\text{observed colligative property}}{\text{calculated colligative property}} > 1\). Common mistake: confusing dissociation (\(i>1\)) with association (\(i<1\)).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Osmotic pressure of the solution can be increased by
questions.optionA: increasing the temperature of the solution
questions.optionB: decreasing the temperature of the solution
questions.optionC: increasing the volume of the vessel
questions.solution: By \(\pi = \frac{n}{V}RT = CRT\), osmotic pressure increases with temperature at constant concentration. Increasing volume or diluting decreases concentration, reducing \(\pi\). Common mistake: thinking dilution or volume increase raises osmotic pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When the pure solvent diffuses out of the solution through the semipermeable membrane then the process is called
questions.solution: In reverse osmosis, excess pressure is applied on the solution side, forcing solvent molecules to flow from solution to pure solvent through a semipermeable membrane. In normal osmosis, solvent flows from pure solvent into solution. Common mistake: confusing osmosis (solvent into solution) with reverse osmosis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The van't Hoff factor 'i' accounts for
questions.solution: Van't Hoff factor \(i = \frac{\text{actual moles of particles in solution}}{\text{moles of solute dissolved}}\), accounting for association or dissociation. Common mistake: confusing dissociation with dissolution (dissolving is a prerequisite, not what \(i\) measures).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Isotonic solutions are having the same
questions.solution: Isotonic solutions have the same osmotic pressure at the same temperature, meaning equal effective molar concentrations. Common mistake: thinking isotonic means same concentration by molarity without considering degree of dissociation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pair of solutions is isotonic?
questions.solution: Effective concentration \(= i \times C\). BaCl\(_2\) (\(i=3\)): \(3 \times 0.01 = 0.03\); NaCl (\(i=2\)): \(2 \times 0.015 = 0.03\). Equal! Common mistake: not multiplying molarity by van't Hoff factor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The \(K_{HI}\) value (kbar) of argon (I), carbon dioxide (II), formaldehyde (III) and methane (IV) are respectively 40.3, 1.67, \(1.83 \times 10^{-5}\) and 0.413 at 298 K. The increasing order of solubility of gas in liquid is
questions.solution: Higher \(K_H\) means lower solubility (\(p = K_H \cdot x\), so larger \(K_H\) needs higher pressure for same mole fraction). Ranking \(K_H\): III (\(1.83\times10^{-5}\)) < II (1.67) < IV (0.413) < I (40.3), so solubility order: I < IV < II < III. Common mistake: thinking higher \(K_H\) means higher solubility.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The vapour pressure of pure liquids A and B are 450 and 700 mm of Hg at 350 K respectively. If the total vapour pressure of the mixture is 600 mm of Hg, the composition of the mixture in the solution is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: An aqueous solution of alcohol contains 18 g of water and 414 g of ethyl alcohol. The mole fraction of water is
questions.solution: Moles of water \(= \frac{18}{18} = 1\); moles of ethanol \(= \frac{414}{46} = 9\). Mole fraction of water \(= \frac{1}{1+9} = 0.1\). Common mistake: using molar mass of methanol (32) instead of ethanol (46).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following colligative properties can provide molar mass of proteins, polymers and colloids with greater precision?
questions.solution: Osmotic pressure measurement gives large, measurable values even for very dilute solutions of high molecular weight substances (\(\pi = CRT\)), making it most suitable for macromolecules. Other colligative properties give very small, difficult-to-measure changes. Common mistake: choosing freezing point depression, which is too small for high molar mass solutes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: If 3 g of glucose (molar mass = 180 g) is dissolved in 60 g of water at 15°C, the osmotic pressure of the solution will be
questions.solution: \(\pi = CRT = \frac{n}{V}RT\). \(n = \frac{3}{180} = 0.01667\) mol; \(V \approx 60 \text{ mL} = 0.06\) L; \(T = 288\) K. \(\pi = \frac{0.01667 \times 0.0821 \times 288}{0.06} \approx 0.657\) atm. Note: Closest answer is 0.65 atm (d). Common mistake: using T in °C instead of K.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Raoult's Law - Moles of Non-volatile Solute
questions.question: At 50°C, the vapour pressure of pure benzene is 268 torr. The number of moles of non-volatile solute per mole of benzene required to prepare a solution having a vapour pressure of 167 torr at the same temperature is
questions.solution: Relative lowering \(= \frac{P^0 - P_s}{P^0} = \frac{268 - 167}{268} = \frac{101}{268} = x_2 = \frac{n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\). With \(n_1 = 1\): \(\frac{n_2}{1 + n_2} = 0.377\), so \(n_2 = \frac{0.377}{0.623} \approx 0.605\). Common mistake: equating relative lowering directly to \(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\) without accounting for total moles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Raoult's Law - Vapour Pressure of Pure Liquid
questions.question: The vapour pressure of a pure liquid A is 70 torr at 300 K. It forms an ideal solution with another liquid B. The mole fraction of B is 0.2 and total vapour pressure of the solution is 84 torr at the same temperature. The vapour pressure of pure liquid B (in torr) is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the freezing point depression constant of a solvent, 50 g of which contain 1 g non volatile solute (molar mass 256 g mol\(^{-1}\)) and the decrease in freezing point is 0.40 K?
questions.solution: \(K_f = \frac{\Delta T_f \times M_2 \times w_1}{w_2 \times 1000} = \frac{0.40 \times 256 \times 50}{1 \times 1000} = \frac{5120}{1000} = 5.12\) K kg mol\(^{-1}\). Common mistake: inverting \(w_1\) and \(w_2\) in the formula.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Graphical Representation of Freezing Point Depression
questions.question: Consider the given plots of vapour pressure (VP) vs temperature (T/K). Which amongst the following options is correct graphical representation showing \(\Delta T_f\), depression in the freezing point of a solvent in a solution?
questions.optionA: Graph (a): Liquid Solvent curve above Solution, freezing point shift left
questions.optionB: Graph (b): Solution curve above Frozen Solvent, freezing point shift right
questions.optionC: Graph (c): Solution and Liquid Solvent crossing with left shift
questions.optionD: Graph (d): Frozen Solution and Liquid Solvent with left shift
questions.solution: In a freezing point depression diagram, the liquid solvent VP curve lies above the solution VP curve. The intersection with solid phase VP shifts to lower temperature (left), showing \(\Delta T_f = T_f^0 - T_f\). Common mistake: thinking the solution VP curve is above the solvent curve.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Rearrange the following sentences in the correct order:
(A) sides like in commercial disputes between big firms, but the
(B) low-wage occupations, is often anything but even
(C) arbitration works well when power is balanced between the two
(D) balance between an employer and an employee, particularly in
questions.solution: The passage contrasts how arbitration works in balanced disputes (C) vs employer-employee situations (A→D→B). C introduces the condition, A continues with contrast, D specifies the context, B completes the idea. A common mistake is placing B before D, breaking the logical flow.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Rearrange the following sentences in the correct order:
(A) ways, in particular by exploiting a "grey zone" where aggression and coercion
(B) neither China nor Russia wants a direct military confrontation with America
(C) that they would surely lose but they are using their growing hard power in other
(D) work just below the level that would risk military confrontation with the West.
questions.solution: B introduces China/Russia's stance, C continues with 'that they would surely lose', A follows with 'in other ways', D concludes with 'grey zone' exploitation. Students often confuse BCAD (D before A) which breaks the flow of 'in other ways ... work just below'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Rearrange the following sentences in the correct order:
(A) companies viable since most of the foreign acquisitions by Indian companies
(B) the world is calculated on the basis of net present value and probable reserves.
(C) are done through loans taken abroad, and valuation of the oil assets all over
(D) the rise in oil prices makes takeovers of oil blocks by Indian oil and natural gas
questions.solution: D begins by stating how rising oil prices benefit Indian companies, C explains acquisitions via loans and valuation, B completes the valuation clause, A provides the reason these companies remain viable. A common error is placing A before D, confusing the subject-predicate relationship.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Rearrange the following sentences in the correct order:
(A) between January and May, outflows from
(B) highest level in 10 years, and by June 4, $6.7 billion was
(C) pulled out on a net basis from the domestic capital market
(D) foreign portfolio investors have reached their
questions.solution: D introduces FPI, B states outflows reached 10-year high with a specific date, A specifies the time period, C concludes with the amount pulled out. Students often start with A (time phrase) rather than D (subject), creating a fragment rather than a complete sentence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Rearrange the following sentences in the correct order:
(A) opted for a hike in key interest rates by 25 basis points
(B) at the end of an unusually long three-day meeting,
(C) which is the first such increase in four and a half years
(D) the Monetary Policy Committee of the Reserve Bank of India
questions.solution: D introduces the MPC as subject, B provides meeting context, A states the decision, C adds a qualifying clause about it being the first in 4.5 years. Students often pick CBDA placing C first, but C is a relative clause and cannot begin the sentence independently.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He tripped **clumsily** on the rug and fell flat on his face. Identify the part of speech of the bold word.
questions.solution: 'Clumsily' modifies the verb 'tripped', describing how the action was done, making it an adverb of manner. Students often confuse adverbs with adjectives; remember adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs, while adjectives modify nouns.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: **Because** she was feeling unwell, she decided to avoid the trip. Identify the part of speech of the bold word.
questions.solution: 'Because' introduces a subordinate clause showing cause/reason, making it a subordinating conjunction. Students often confuse it with an adverb since 'because' answers 'why'; however, it connects two clauses, which is the defining role of a conjunction.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: It's never too late to be what you **might** have been. Identify the part of speech of the bold word.
questions.solution: 'Might' is a modal auxiliary verb used to express possibility; it helps the main verb 'have been' form the perfect modal construction. Students often select 'Intransitive verb' since 'might' cannot take an object, but its role here is as a helping verb, not a main verb.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The student **borrowed** a pen from her classmate as she had forgotten to get her own pen. Identify the part of speech of the bold word.
questions.solution: 'Borrowed' takes a direct object ('a pen'), making it a transitive verb. A common mistake is choosing 'Past tense' — while 'borrowed' is indeed past tense, past tense is a grammatical form, not a part of speech.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Everyone **had gone** home by the time she reached the party. Identify the part of speech of the bold words.
questions.solution: 'Had gone' is the past perfect tense form, used to indicate an action completed before another past action. Students confuse this with 'Past tense' (simple past), but the presence of 'had' signals past perfect, not simple past.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage on Symbiosis, Remora attaches itself to the shark or whale:
questions.optionA: by entwining its long body around the bigger fish.
questions.optionB: by biting into the fish's body with its teeth.
questions.optionC: with an adhesive organ found in its head.
questions.optionD: with a hook-like structure in its head.
questions.solution: The passage states the remora 'uses a sucker on its head' to attach itself. Option C ('adhesive organ') correctly paraphrases 'sucker'. Students often choose D ('hook-like structure') by confusing a sucker (adhesion-based) with a hook (piercing-based).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Commensal relationship is a type of symbiosis in which the relationship is beneficial:
questions.optionA: to one and harmless to other.
questions.optionB: to one and harmful to others.
questions.optionD: to both for a very short time.
questions.solution: The passage defines commensalism as 'one partner gains a benefit while the other gains little or none but is not harmed'. Option A correctly captures 'beneficial to one, harmless to other'. Students often confuse commensalism with mutualism (beneficial to both).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The passage on Symbiosis talks about how animals:
questions.optionB: are not related to each other.
questions.optionC: take advantage of the weaker ones.
questions.solution: The central theme of the passage is 'Symbiosis' meaning 'living together', covering all types of relationships including commensalism, parasitism, and mutualism. Students may choose A (help each other) which only describes mutualism, not the full scope of the passage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, Parasites:
questions.optionA: are neither beneficial nor harmful to animals they are with.
questions.optionB: benefit at the expense of the animals they live with.
questions.optionC: are beneficial to the animals they live with.
questions.optionD: love the animals they live with.
questions.solution: The passage defines parasitism as 'one partner benefits at the expense of others'. Option B is the direct match. Students often confuse parasitism with commensalism (option A), mixing up which relationship is neutral vs. harmful.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, Remora feeds:
questions.optionA: on the shark it travels with.
questions.optionB: on the left-over parts of the shark's prey.
questions.optionC: by detaching itself to attack the prey.
questions.optionD: on a whale or another type of fish
questions.solution: The passage states 'When the shark makes a kill, the hitchhiker briefly detaches itself to feed on the scraps.' Option B correctly states it feeds on leftover scraps. Students confuse C (attacks prey) with B; the remora feeds on scraps, not attacks prey independently.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What does the idiom "Threw up my cards" mean?
questions.optionA: put my whole income at stake
questions.solution: 'Threw up my cards' is an idiom from card games meaning to abandon one's hand/strategy, hence 'gave up my plan'. Students often choose D ('gambled all my money') by taking the literal card-game imagery too seriously.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What does the idiom "Feather someone's nest" mean?
questions.solution: 'Feather someone's nest' means to enrich or provide for one's own comfort/needs, often at others' expense. Students often pick B (decorate home) by interpreting the phrase too literally, missing the figurative meaning of self-enrichment.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What does the idiom "A chip of the old block" mean?
questions.optionA: a being of the old generation
questions.optionC: a common boy of the locality
questions.optionD: someone similar in character to one's father
questions.solution: 'A chip off the old block' refers to a person who resembles their parent in character or behaviour. Students often confuse it with A (old generation) taking the word 'old' literally rather than understanding it refers to parental similarity.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What does the idiom "An arm-chair critic" mean?
questions.optionA: someone who gives advice based on theory not practice
questions.optionB: someone who gives advice based on practice not theory
questions.optionC: someone who gives advice based on experience
questions.solution: An 'armchair critic' refers to someone who offers criticism or advice from a position of comfort without any practical experience. Students often choose C (based on experience) but the idiom specifically implies lack of hands-on experience, not presence of it.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What does the idiom "French leave" mean?
questions.solution: 'French leave' means to leave a place or event without informing anyone or taking formal leave. Students confuse A (absent without permission) with B; while both involve informality, 'French leave' specifically means departing without saying goodbye or informing others.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: He achieved his position through **chicanery**, not merit.
questions.solution: 'Chicanery' means using trickery or deception to achieve one's purpose. 'Trickery' is the correct synonym. Students often guess A (Honesty) as an antonym when asked for a synonym, or choose B/D by confusing the word with related character traits.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: Everyone **extolled** her for her generosity.
questions.solution: 'Extolled' means to praise enthusiastically or highly. 'Praised' is the direct synonym. Students sometimes pick A (Criticised) as it's the most familiar antonym of praise, confusing synonym with antonym under time pressure.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: The spread of fake news has a **pernicious** effect on society.
questions.solution: 'Pernicious' means causing insidious harm or death, especially gradually. 'Deadly' or 'harmful' is the correct synonym. Students often pick A (Beneficial) which is the antonym, or B (Harmless), failing to recognize the word's strong negative connotation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: She is extremely **fastidious** about keeping her room clean.
questions.solution: 'Fastidious' means very attentive to accuracy and detail; hard to please. Option B is correct. Students often choose A (Careless), the antonym, or C/D by guessing based on unfamiliarity with the word.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: Bravery was the most **salient** feature of his character.
questions.solution: 'Salient' means most noticeable or important; prominent. 'Noticeable' is the synonym. Students may choose A (Hidden) as the opposite, confusing synonym with antonym, or B/D by misinterpreting 'salient' as negative.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: Her **winsome** smile impressed everyone at the event.
questions.solution: 'Winsome' means attractive or appealing in appearance or character. 'Charming' is the best synonym. Students often pick A (Fake) by associating 'winsome' with 'win' in a negative/manipulative sense, misunderstanding the word's positive connotation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: The **moribund** company was on the verge of shutting down.
questions.solution: 'Moribund' means at the point of death or in terminal decline. 'Dying' is the synonym. Students frequently choose A (Thriving) as an antonym, or D (Restructuring) by associating decline with restructuring activity.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: The **recalcitrant** student refused to obey school rules.
questions.solution: 'Recalcitrant' means stubbornly resisting authority or discipline; refractory. 'Refractory' is the precise synonym. Students often choose B (Angry) confusing emotional state with behavioural resistance, or A (Obedient) which is the antonym.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: After the meeting, he became unusually **garrulous** and kept talking nonstop.
questions.solution: 'Garrulous' means excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters. 'Talkative' is the synonym. Context clue 'kept talking nonstop' also confirms B. Students sometimes pick A (Silent) as the antonym, confused by the unusual word.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the synonym of the bold word: The customer care centre was **inundated** with complaints after the outage.
questions.solution: 'Inundated' means overwhelmed with things to be dealt with, or literally flooded. 'Flooded' is the more precise synonym. Students often choose B (Filled) which is close but lacks the sense of overwhelming excess that 'inundated' implies.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: She decided to **relinquish** her position to focus on her studies.
questions.solution: 'Relinquish' means to give up or surrender. Its antonym is 'Retain' (to keep). Students often pick A (Abandon) or B (Surrender) which are synonyms of relinquish, not antonyms — a classic mistake when question asks for antonym but synonyms are listed.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: The country went through a **turbulent** period marked by protests.
questions.solution: 'Turbulent' means full of disorder or confusion. Its antonym is 'Calm'. Students frequently choose B (Violent) or C (Chaotic) which are synonyms of turbulent, not antonyms, especially when under time pressure.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: It is **prudent** to save money for emergencies rather than spend it all.
questions.solution: 'Prudent' means acting with or showing care and thought for the future; wise. Its antonym is 'Reckless' (careless of consequences). Students often pick A (Sensible) or C/D which are synonyms of prudent.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: Trekking through the dense forest was an **arduous** experience.
questions.solution: 'Arduous' means involving or requiring strenuous effort; difficult. Its antonym is 'Easy'. Students commonly select A (Strenuous), C (Tough), or D (Exhausting), all of which are synonyms of arduous rather than opposites.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: His **impeccable** manners impressed everyone at the event.
questions.solution: 'Impeccable' means in accordance with the highest standards; faultless. Its antonym is 'Defective'. Students often choose A (Faultless) which is a synonym of impeccable, confusing the direction of meaning when the question asks for antonym.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: The **amorphous** cloud of smoke spread across the sky.
questions.solution: 'Amorphous' means without a clearly defined shape or form. Its antonym is 'Structured'. Students often choose A (Formless), C (Vague), or D (Undefined), all synonyms of amorphous, when the question requires the opposite meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: He often wastes time discussing **trivial** matters at work.
questions.solution: 'Trivial' means of little value or importance. Its antonym is 'Important'. Students frequently choose A (Minor) or B (Insignificant) or D (Negligible), all of which are synonyms of trivial, reversing the direction of the question.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: She gave a **candid** reply about her mistakes.
questions.solution: 'Candid' means truthful and straightforward; frank. Its antonym is 'Secretive'. Students often pick A (Direct) or B (Sincere) or D (Open), all synonyms, mistaking the synonym options for antonyms.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: The leader delivered a **profound** speech that moved the audience.
questions.solution: 'Profound' means having or showing great knowledge or insight; deep. Its antonym is 'Shallow'. Students may choose B (Meaningful), C (Intense), or D (Serious) as they associate these with impactful speeches, but only A directly opposes the meaning of 'profound'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the antonym of the bold word: The criminal remained **elusive** despite several attempts to trace him.
questions.solution: 'Elusive' means difficult to find, catch, or achieve. Its antonym is 'Obvious' (easily found or understood). Students often select A (Slippery) or B (Evasive) which are synonyms of elusive, or D (Mysterious) which is related but not an antonym.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: Your success in the IAS examinations depends not only on (a)/ what papers you have selected (b)/ but on how you have written them. (c) No error. (d)
questions.optionA: Your success in the IAS examinations depends not only on
questions.optionC: but on how you have written them.
questions.solution: The correlative conjunction 'not only...but also' requires 'also' after 'but'. The correct form is 'but also on how you have written them'. Students often miss the missing 'also' since 'but on' sounds natural in spoken English.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: Heavy rain (a)/ prevented us (b)/ to go to the cinema. (c)/ No error. (d)
questions.solution: The verb 'prevent' is followed by 'from + gerund' (prevent us from going), not the infinitive. The correct phrase is 'prevented us from going to the cinema'. Students confuse 'prevent' with verbs like 'allow/permit' which take infinitives.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: He asked (a)/ supposing if he fails (b)/ what he would do. (c)/ No error. (d)
questions.solution: 'Supposing' and 'if' both introduce a conditional; using both together is redundant. It should be either 'supposing he fails' or 'if he fails'. Students overlook this as both words separately seem grammatically acceptable.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: We had a lot of difficulty (a)/ to find (b)/ the house. (c)/ No error. (d).
questions.optionA: We had a lot of difficulty
questions.solution: The phrase 'difficulty' must be followed by 'in + gerund' (difficulty in finding), not the infinitive. Correct form: 'We had a lot of difficulty in finding the house'. Students confuse difficulty (gerund) with ability/inability constructions (infinitive).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: Patience as well as perseverance (a)/ are necessary (b)/ for success. (c)/ No error/(d).
questions.solution: When subjects are connected by 'as well as', the verb agrees with the first subject ('Patience'), which is singular, requiring 'is necessary'. 'As well as' is a parenthetical addition, not a conjunction like 'and'. Students incorrectly treat it as 'and', expecting a plural verb.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: The passer-by told us (a)/ where was the marriage hall (b)/ and even led us to it. (c)/ No error/(d).
questions.optionC: and even led us to it.
questions.solution: In indirect speech (reported speech), the word order follows the assertive sentence structure: subject before verb. Correct form is 'where the marriage hall was'. Students forget to invert word order back to normal when converting to indirect speech.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: The increase in consumption is directly (a)/ proportional to the increase (b)/ in income. (c)/No error. (d).
questions.optionA: The increase in consumption is directly
questions.solution: The sentence is grammatically correct. 'Directly proportional to' is the standard phrase used in mathematics and economics. Students may incorrectly flag 'in income' as an error, but 'increase in income' is idiomatic and correct.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: In Singapore (a)/ my brother-in-law with his wife (b)/ were present at the function. (c)/No error. (d).
questions.solution: The subject 'my brother-in-law' is singular; 'with his wife' is a parenthetical phrase, not a conjunction. So the verb should be 'was present', not 'were present'. Students treat 'with' as equivalent to 'and', making the subject appear plural.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: Scarcely had (a)/ I arrived when (b)/ the train left. (c)/No error. (d).
questions.solution: 'Scarcely' is paired with 'before', not 'when'. Correct form: 'Scarcely had I arrived before the train left.' Similarly, 'hardly...when/before' are paired. Students confuse 'no sooner...than' (correct) with 'scarcely...when' (incorrect).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Spot the error: He is one of those writers (a)/ who has won (b)/ acclaim worldwide.(c)/No error (d).
questions.optionA: He is one of those writers
questions.solution: The relative pronoun 'who' refers to 'writers' (plural), so the verb should be 'who have won', not 'who has won'. The antecedent of 'who' is the plural 'writers', not the singular 'He'. Students are misled by the proximity of 'He' to 'who'.
